A woman on a subway platform is arguing into her phone, sharp and frustrated, her free hand cutting the air. The train arrives. She steps on, still mid-sentence, and finds a seat across from a child—maybe four years old—who is holding a tangerine.

The child is not eating the tangerine. She is just holding it with both hands and looking at it with an intensity that suggests she has recently discovered that tangerines exist.

The woman's voice falters. Not because of the child exactly, but because something in the quality of the child's attention has leaked into the air of the train car. The tangerine is very orange. Stupidly, impossibly orange against the grey-blue of the seats and the smudged silver of the poles and the particular exhausted yellow of the overhead lighting.

The woman says "I'll call you back" and hangs up.

She doesn't look at the child again. She looks at her own hands. The train moves. The child turns the tangerine over slowly, discovering that the other side is also orange.

Nothing else happens. The moment doesn't mean anything. The woman gets off at the next stop and returns to her life and its arguments. The child will eat the tangerine or won't.

But for a few seconds, the orange was that orange, and someone was paying the kind of attention that made it visible, and someone else noticed, and the noticing made a small rip in the ordinary way of things.

That's all.
